bioHot › MS1
← bioHot

Compartmentalized Co-metabolism in the Porcine Gut: Systematic Evidence Mapping and a Theoretical Framework for Epigenetic–Microbiome Interactions

Liu Haoan, Feng Wen*

Yulin University, Yulin, Shaanxi 719000, China · wfeng@yulinu.edu.cn · ORCID: 0000-0001-8485-5345 (W.F.), 0009-0008-1764-0908 (H.L.)

Manuscript in preparation · Full text available on request

Abstract

Background. The epigenome–microbiome axis is supported by substantial evidence for bidirectional interactions between gut microbes and host epigenetics; however, a theoretical framework that explicitly integrates spatial organization with temporal dynamics has not yet been proposed. The "epigenome–microbiome axis" (Pepke et al.) and the "MDME axis" (Lei et al.) have established the forward and reverse pathways as a foundational paradigm. Neither framework accounts for spatial compartmentalization or the temporal dynamics of metabolite concentration gradients across the gut.

Methods. We construct the Compartmentalized Co-metabolism Interaction Network Model, specifying three functionally distinct intestinal compartments (luminal, epithelial, microenvironment) with their associated metabolite concentration ranges and temporal dynamics. We develop a Four-Dimensional Matrix Rating System (Forward Pathway, Reverse Pathway, Coupling Verification Depth, Measurement Depth) as a diagnostic tool for evidence mapping. A systematic evidence mapping following PRISMA-ScR guidelines was conducted across PubMed, CNKI, and Wanfang Data, with 26 studies included in qualitative synthesis.

Results. The forward pathway (microbes → metabolites → host epigenetics) is well-validated in porcine models, primarily through the SCFAs–HDAC–barrier axis. The reverse pathway (host epigenetics → microbial regulation) lacks direct causal validation in porcine models. A universal and critical finding: all 22 empirical studies scored Level 0 on Measurement Depth (Dimension IV), indicating no study has simultaneously measured metabolites and epigenetic marks across multiple compartments and multiple timepoints. Shared metabolites (butyrate/SCFAs, bile acids, tryptophan metabolites, polyamines, vitamin B12) act as interaction nodes with forward effects partially validated but reverse roles entirely unexplored.

Conclusions. The model shifts the paradigm from a "bidirectional axis" to a "compartmentalized co-metabolism network," generating four testable, falsifiable predictions with specific numerical thresholds. A Research Priority Matrix (P1–P3) guides community validation efforts, with butyrate at the lumen–epithelium interface identified as the minimum verifiable unit (P1, 2–3 yr timeline).

1. The Compartmentalized Co-Metabolism Network Model

The model divides the porcine gut into three functionally distinct but interconnected compartments. The compartments are conceptual constructs, not strict anatomical divisions, acknowledging that biological parameters exist along continuous gradients. A molecule has different concentrations, functions, and dynamics in each compartment; interactions occur only at compartment interfaces.

Luminal Compartment

Site of microbial metabolism. SCFAs produced at millimolar concentrations (butyrate ~4–20 mM in porcine cecal and proximal colonic contents). Carrier-independent passive diffusion across the apical membrane. Timescale: minutes to hours.

Epithelial Compartment

Site of host epigenetic regulation. Intracellular butyrate concentrations ~3 orders of magnitude lower than luminal (micromolar range). MCT1-mediated active transport across the apical membrane; majority of imported butyrate is rapidly metabolically consumed. Timescale: hours to days.

Microenvironment Compartment

Site of feedback pathways. Host-secreted immune factors (IgA, defensins, cytokines), pH, oxygen concentration, and mucus layer thickness. Chemical and physical gradients regulated by both microbial metabolism and host epithelial activity. Timescale: days to weeks.

Core principle: The forward and reverse pathways are not independent causal chains but are connected through shared metabolites acting as interaction nodes bridging compartments at concentrations differing by three orders of magnitude and on timescales differing by days. This spatial and temporal asynchrony may explain inconsistent SCFA intervention effects in weaned piglets.

2. Four-Dimensional Matrix Rating System

All operational definitions below are reproduced from Supplementary Table S3. Dimensions I–III are scored Level 0–4 (five levels); Dimension IV is scored Level 0–2 (three levels), reflecting the current technological ceiling.

DimensionLevelOperational Definition
I. Forward Pathway
(Microbe → Host)		0No study has tested whether microbial metabolites regulate host epigenetic marks in any model system.
1Association inferred from co-occurrence network analysis or statistical correlation in porcine gut metagenomic/metabolomic data.
2aIn vitro porcine model validation (e.g., porcine ileal organoids, IPEC-J2 cells) demonstrating that specific metabolites regulate epigenetic marks.
2bIn vivo porcine model validation (e.g., oral metabolite supplementation) demonstrating that metabolites regulate epigenetic marks in the porcine gut.
3Causal validation in murine models (e.g., mono-colonization, gene knockout) confirming the forward pathway causal chain.
4Causal validation in porcine models with independent replication by a separate laboratory.
II. Reverse Pathway
(Host → Microbiome)		0No study has tested whether host epigenetic status regulates the gut microbiota in any model system.
1Association inferred from host genetic–microbiome correlation studies, maternal nutritional programming studies, or germ-free pig transcriptome comparisons.
2aIn vitro porcine model validation demonstrating that host epigenetic changes alter microbial community composition or function.
2bIn vivo porcine model validation (e.g., HDAC inhibitor treatment, dietary methyl-donor manipulation) demonstrating that host epigenetic changes alter the gut microbiota.
3Causal validation in murine models (e.g., SIRT1, HDAC6, Tet2, or KDM5 knockout) confirming the reverse pathway causal chain.
4Causal validation in porcine models with independent replication by a separate laboratory.
III. Coupling Verification Depth0Neither the forward nor the reverse pathway has been verified for this node in any model system.
1Forward and reverse pathways have been independently verified, but not within the same experimental system.
2Forward and reverse effects have been simultaneously observed within the same experiment, but without interventional proof of causal connection between the two pathways.
3An intervention experiment demonstrates that blocking one pathway (e.g., forward) affects the effect of the other pathway (e.g., reverse), establishing causal coupling.
4Cross-scale coupling verified through simultaneous multi-compartment, multi-timepoint measurement within a single experiment, demonstrating that forward effects temporally precede reverse effects.
IV. Measurement Depth0Single compartment measured at a single timepoint; no simultaneous measurement of metabolites and epigenetic marks across multiple compartments.
1Multiple compartments measured OR multiple timepoints sampled.
2Multiple compartments measured AND multiple timepoints sampled within a single experimental system.

3. Key Findings

Universal Measurement Depth Deficiency. All 22 empirical studies (20 from systematic search + 2 supplementary in vitro studies) scored Level 0 on Measurement Depth. No study has simultaneously measured metabolites and epigenetic marks across multiple compartments and multiple timepoints. This is not a failure of individual studies but reflects a systemic methodological gap: the field has not yet adopted multi-compartment, multi-timepoint experimental designs.
Systematic Evidence Asymmetry. The forward pathway (SCFAs → HDAC inhibition → histone acetylation → intestinal barrier maintenance) carries in vivo validation evidence in porcine models (Level 2b), including the landmark study by Xiong et al. (2016). The reverse pathway—defined as experimental manipulation of a host epigenetic enzyme followed by measurement of gut microbiota compositional change—remains entirely absent from the porcine literature (Level 0). Shared metabolites (butyrate/SCFAs, bile acids, tryptophan metabolites, polyamines, vitamin B12) have had forward effects partially validated, while reverse roles remain unexplored. Interaction nodes represent the most unbalanced region in the current evidence distribution.

4. Research Priority Matrix & Falsifiable Predictions

PriorityNodeFeasibilityImportanceKey ExperimentTimeline
P1Butyrate (lumen–epithelium interface)HighHighIntracellular butyrate → HDAC → histone acetylation, with simultaneous luminal + epithelial + microenvironment sampling2–3 yr
P2Bile acids; Tryptophan metabolitesMediumHighFXR/TGR5 and AhR pathway validation with multi-compartment sampling—
P3Polyamines; Vitamin B12LowMediumCross-species extrapolation needed; porcine models absent at any level for polyamines and B12—
Falsifiable prediction (core): Intracellular butyrate concentration is a better predictor of host epigenetic changes than luminal butyrate concentration. Specifically: (a) intracellular butyrate concentration is predicted to correlate with H3K9 acetylation at r ≥ 0.7, while (b) luminal butyrate concentration is predicted to correlate with H3K9 acetylation at r ≤ 0.3. The core claim—that intracellular concentration is the better predictor—survives the failure of any specific numerical threshold; the thresholds are empirically informed benchmarks (Xiong et al. 2016 effect sizes) proposed as testable hypotheses.

5. Methods

Systematic evidence mapping following PRISMA-ScR guidelines. Primary search: PubMed (MEDLINE), May 20, 2026 (n = 12 records). Supplementary searches: CNKI and Wanfang Data (Chinese-language studies). Reference list checking (snowballing) identified 8 additional empirical studies and 6 framework/review articles. After deduplication, 26 studies were included in qualitative synthesis (20 empirical + 6 framework/review). Three reviewers with doctoral-level expertise in porcine gut microbiome or epigenetics independently completed the matrix rating. Complete search strategies and the PRISMA-ScR checklist are provided in the Supplementary Information (available on request).

6. Relationship to MS2

This manuscript (MS2) provides a complementary framework. MS1 operates at the metabolite-node level (diagnosing evidence distribution across a co-metabolism network), while MS2 operates at the strain level (assessing translational readiness of specific probiotic candidates). Together they form the methodological foundation for the Gut Microbiome Co-Metabolism Evidence Monitor channel on bioHot.
